original_text:
### Editorial / Style Rules
- **Abbreviation "DM":** After first mention of "dermatomyositis," use "DM" in running copy to avoid repetition. **Do NOT use "DM" in headlines, headings, or subheads** — always the full word there (clarity, accessibility, and SEO on web). First-mention pattern: `dermatomyositis (DM)`.
  - Because sections are assembled later: **[GENERAL] every standalone section must establish `dermatomyositis (DM)` at its own first mention** unless the assembly brief states the abbreviation is established upstream.
- **Inclusive language (patient materials):** say "someone with dermatomyositis" / "people with dermatomyositis" — **not "patients."** ("Patients" is acceptable only in HCP materials.) Use **"care team"** (not "staff") for the network of patients, HCPs, and support systems.
- **Numbers:** spell out zero–nine; numerals for 10+. **Exceptions — always numerals: percentages, ages, ratios.** Percentages use the % sign (e.g., 74%).
- **Data tone:** when citing clinical data, avoid negative or fear-based language; use an empathetic, supportive, trust-building tone without oversimplifying. Example pattern: "Participants in the VALOR study taking LISRAYA had nearly 50% more improvement in their DM symptoms after one year compared to placebo."
- **Punctuation:** Oxford/serial comma always in series of 3+.

relations.note: Adds the standalone-section assembly requirement to the general first-mention abbreviation pattern.

Do NOT use "DM" in headlines, headings, or subheads** — always the full word there

rules.intent: Establish a clear, accessible abbreviation while preserving full-term clarity in display copy.
rules.rule_text: At first mention, write “dermatomyositis (DM)”; use “DM” in running copy after that first mention to avoid repetition. Do not use “DM” in headlines, headings, or subheads; use the full word there for clarity, accessibility, and web SEO.

rules.intent: Use inclusive, person-first language appropriate to the intended audience.

rules.intent: Maintain consistent, readable numeric editorial style.
rules.rule_text: Spell out zero through nine and use numerals for 10 and above. Always use numerals for percentages, ages, and ratios; express percentages with the % sign, such as 74%.

rules.intent: Communicate clinical information supportively while retaining appropriate nuance.
rules.rule_text: When citing clinical data, avoid negative or fear-based language. Use an empathetic, supportive, trust-building tone without oversimplifying.

rules.intent: Maintain consistent punctuation and improve list clarity.
rules.rule_text: Always use the Oxford/serial comma in a series of three or more items.

- **Data tone:** when citing clinical data, avoid negative or fear-based language; use an empathetic, supportive, trust-building tone without oversimplifying. Example pattern: "Participants in the VALOR study taking LISRAYA had nearly 50% more improvement in their DM symptoms after one year compared to placebo."
- **Punctuation:** Oxford/serial comma always in series of 3+.

rules.rule_text:
The Editorial / Style Rules table is a closed, brand-approved set; every row binds its listed values exactly as specified. Do not invent or alter rows.

| item | value |
|---|---|

| Data tone | ** when citing clinical data, avoid negative or fear-based language; use an empathetic, supportive, trust-building tone without oversimplifying. Example pattern: "Participants in the VALOR study taking LISRAYA had nearly 50% more improvement in their DM symptoms after one year compared to placebo." |
| Punctuation | ** Oxford/serial comma always in series of 3+. |
